text: This video demonstrates the technique of laparoscopic nascent fundoplication in f.x speed with minimal editing. Our selection criteria are shown here. They are a patient with typical reflex symptoms, objective evidence of reflux and good response to PPI. This patient is a twenty-three year old gentleman with recurrent episodes of heartburn and regurgitation and grade C esophagitis of endoscopy with a large hiatus hernia. On manometry, the esophageal motility was acceptable and by definition did not fall in the ineffective esophageal motility category. The operative team setup and port positions are shown in the figure.

The wide hiatus and hiatus hernia are visible. The surgeon adopts the left crus approach, beginning with the division of short gastric vessels and identifying the left crus. The assistant lifts the greater omentum near the lower pole of the spleen, while the surgeon applies counter-traction on the stomach near the greater curvature, starting the division of the omentum close to the stomach. Care is taken to avoid injury to the greater curvature and posterior fundus, with traction and counter-traction adjustments to maintain exposure of the operative field.

The surgeon grasps the posterior fundus near the upper pole of the spleen, while the assistant retracts the posterior stomach caudally and medially. The posterior gastric vessels and attachments of the posterior fundus to the left crus are divided liberally.

The surgeon dissects the hernia sac away from the stomach and esophagus, dividing it to gain esophageal length. The phrenosophageal membrane is divided, while ventral traction on the stomach is maintained to expose the retroperitoneal attachments of the fundus.

The surgeon divides the sac attached to the esophagus, revealing a well-developed sac due to the patient's large hiatus hernia. A gauze piece is placed at the base of the left crus for identification from the right side. The assistant retracts the fundus in the specified direction, and the past flaccida is divided.

The surgeon makes an incision at the base of the right crus, facilitating straightforward looping of the esophagus. The dissection of the sac continues on the right side to release it from the esophagus, while avoiding entry into the mediastinum or left pleura.

The surgeon performs circumferential mediastinal esophageal mobilization to gain intra-abdominal length, using alternating traction on the tape placed around the esophagus to enhance exposure for the dissection.

The surgeon assesses the adequacy of mediastinal esophageal mobilization by visualizing the left crus from the right side and vice versa. The shoe shine maneuver is performed to evaluate the tension and adequacy of the wrap. The fundus remains stable, indicating effective posterior immobilization. Chloroplasty is performed using 2-0 Ethibond sutures on a double-arm needle, with threads divided on both sides, maintaining a length of approximately twenty centimeters.

After completing the crural repair, the surgeon passes a gastric calibration tube across the gastroesophageal junction to simulate a food bolus, ensuring the wrap is not excessively tight. Caudal traction is applied to the tape around the GI junction, positioning the wrap around the lower esophagus above the junction, avoiding placement at the cardia to prevent dysphagia and potential funnel wrap migration.
The surgeon demonstrates the correct orientation of the suture line of the wrap at eleven o'clock and the cooler closure at one o'clock, ensuring proper alignment of the surgical repair.
